comprehensive_summary:
Sappho is an ancient Greek lyric poet from the island of Lesbos, widely known as the 'Tenth Muse.' She is one of the nine canonical Greek lyric poets and its sole female member. Her works survive almost entirely in fragments, with the 'Hymn to Aphrodite' (Fragment 1) being her only complete surviving poem. Anne Carson's If Not, Winter is the standard modern translation, notable for its liberal use of square brackets to indicate lacunae in the original text.

The 'Hymn to Aphrodite' (Fragment 1) opens with the phrase 'Deathless Aphrodite of the spangled mind' and describes a chariot drawn by sparrows (or doves, depending on translation). The speaker addresses the goddess as 'iridescent-throned,' 'child of Zeus,' and asks her to 'be my ally' and not to 'break with hard pains my heart.' Aphrodite asks 'Whom this time should I persuade to lead you back again to her love?'

Fragment 31 (also called 'Ode to Anactoria' or 'phainetai moi') opens with 'He seems to me equal to the gods,' describing a man who sits opposite a woman listening to her 'sweet speaking.' The speaker experiences physical symptoms of desire: tongue breaks, 'thin fire racing under skin,' feeling 'greener than grass' (or 'paler than grass,' depending on translation), cold sweat, and seems 'nearly to have died.' The poem was quoted by Longinus in On the Sublime as an example of literary intensity. It was adapted by Catullus as Poem 51. The poem abruptly ends mid-sentence with 'But all is to be dared, because even a person of poverty.'

The 'Brothers Poem' was rediscovered in 2014 by a team led by Dirk Obbink. It centers on Sappho's brothers Charaxos and Larichos, with the speaker wishing that one brother would 'raise up his head and become a man.'

Fragment 58 (the 'Tithonus poem') recalls the mythological story of Tithonus and describes skin that was 'once supple and smooth.' Carson translated it as 'The Beat Goes On' after additional lines were found at the University of Cologne. The poem states 'a human being without old age is not a possibility.'

The 'midnight poem,' long attributed to Sappho, describes the speaker sleeping alone when 'the moon and the Pleiades have set' and 'the time is passing.' A.E. Housman wrote several adaptations of it.

Sappho also wrote a poem about a wedding that repeats 'O Hymenaeus!' and proclaims 'Lift high the roofbeam, carpenters.' She wrote 'The Marriage of Hektor and Andromache' and a poem in which a girl declares 'Mother dear, I can't finish my weaving.' She predicted 'not one girl I think / who looks on the light of the sun / will ever / have wisdom / like this' and boasted 'Although they are only breath, words which I command are immortal.' In a poem sometimes titled 'To an army wife in Sardis,' the speaker argues that the 'most beautiful thing on the black earth' is not an army of horses or ships but 'what you love,' citing Helen leaving her child and parents for Troy.

Sappho wrote in the Aeolic dialect and lends her name to the Sapphic stanza form, consisting of three hendecasyllable lines capped by a five-syllable Adonic line. She was a contemporary of Alcaeus of Mytilene, who praised her as having a 'honey-sweet smile.' Her name on the island of Lesbos is the origin of the term 'lesbian.'

Biographically, legend holds that Sappho threw herself from the Leucadian cliffs due to unrequited love for the ferryman Phaon. This story first appeared in a play by Menander. She was exiled in Sicily according to the Marmor Parium. Her purported daughter Cleis is sometimes interpreted as a homosexual lover instead. Ulrich von Wilamowitz-Moellendorff theorized she was head of a girls' boarding school. Franz Grillparzer wrote a tragedy in which Sappho believes her slave Melitta has seduced the youth she loves. Algernon Swinburne wrote the dramatic monologue 'Anactoria' and a sadomasochistic poem in Poems and Ballads from Sappho's perspective. Renee Vivien published a translation of Sappho in 1903. Judy Chicago referenced Sappho as 'the flower of the Graces' in The Dinner Party. Giacomo Leopardi wrote 'The Last Song of Sappho.' Anne Carson's book Eros the Bittersweet claims Sappho first called eros 'bittersweet.'

works.name: Hymn to Aphrodite (Fragment 1)
works.description: Sappho's only complete surviving poem. Addressed to Aphrodite, described as 'Deathless' and 'of the spangled mind,' with a chariot drawn by sparrows. The speaker asks the goddess to be her ally in love.

clues.clue: Opens 'Deathless Aphrodite of the spangled mind'
quotes: The phrase 'Deathless Aphrodite of the spangled mind' opens the 'Ode to Aphrodite'
Commands like 'take the bitter care from my mind' and 'in all my battles fight as my comrade' end a poem by this author addressed to a 'deathless' and 'iridescent-throned' goddess

quotes: ends by asking the title figure to 'be my ally'

quotes: addressed to a 'deathless' and 'iridescent-throned' goddess

quotes: a dedicatee who asked 'Whom this time should I persuade to lead you back again to her love?'

works.name: Fragment 31 (Ode to Anactoria / phainetai moi)
works.description: Opens 'He seems to me equal to the gods,' describing a man sitting opposite a woman. The speaker experiences intense physical symptoms of desire. Quoted by Longinus in On the Sublime and adapted by Catullus as Poem 51.

a poem that describes a man who 'opposite you / sits and listens close / to your sweet speaking'

quotes: feels 'thin fire...racing under skin' in Anne Carson's translation

clues.clue: Adapted by Catullus as Poem 51
quotes: That poem was later adapted as Catullus's Poem 51

quotes: This poem abruptly ends mid-sentence with the line 'But all is to be dared, because even a person of poverty'

works.name: If Not, Winter (Anne Carson translation)
works.description: Anne Carson's comprehensive translation of all Sappho's extant works and fragments, using square brackets to indicate lacunae. The title comes from Fragment 22.

clues.clue: Anne Carson's translation of Sappho using square brackets as placeholders for lost/undecipherable words
quotes: Anne Carson's If Not, Winter is a translation of this poet

clues.clue: Title comes from Fragment 22
quotes: A line from Fragment 22 titles, for 10 points, what collection of Sappho translations by Anne Carson?

works.name: Other Poems
works.description: Various other fragments and poems, including the 'midnight poem,' 'To an army wife in Sardis,' wedding poems, and 'Mother dear, I can't finish my weaving.'

clues.clue: 'To an army wife in Sardis': the most beautiful thing on earth is 'what you love' / 'whoever one loves'; cites Helen leaving for Troy
quotes: others consider cavalry, foot soldiers, or fleets to be the most beautiful sights, but the author thinks it's 'whatever you love best'
the 'most beautiful thing / on the black earth' is 'what you love'

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Ancient Greek poet from the island of Lesbos, known as the 'Tenth Muse.' She wrote in the Aeolic dialect and lends her name to the Sapphic stanza form. Her sexuality and biography are subjects of extensive speculation.

clues.clue: Known as the 'Tenth Muse'
quotes: this island home to the 'tenth muse,' the Greek poet Sappho
this ancient Greek 'tenth Muse'

clues.clue: From the island of Lesbos; origin of the term 'lesbian'
quotes: Sappho is from what island, whose name is the origin of the term for female same-sex love?
this poet from Lesbos

clues.clue: Names the Sapphic stanza: three hendecasyllable lines capped by a five-syllable Adonic line
quotes: A metrical form named for this poet is notable for its 'Adonic line,' which consists of a dactyl and a spondee
names a type of stanza in which a five-syllable ending caps off three hendecasyllable lines

clues.clue: Wrote in the Aeolic dialect
quotes: This poet wrote in the Aeolic dialect

clues.clue: Legend: threw herself from the Leucadian cliffs for ferryman Phaon; story first in Menander
quotes: this woman supposedly jumped off of to kill herself due to her unrequited love for the ferryman Phaon

clues.clue: Contemporary of Alcaeus of Mytilene, who praised her 'honey-sweet smile'
quotes: a contemporary of the poet Alceus
a fragmentary poem praises this poet as 'you with strands of hair in violet, O holy one, you with the honey-sweet smile'

clues.clue: One of nine canonical Greek lyric poets; sole female member

quotes: Admirers called this poet 'the flower of the Graces,' a name referenced in The Dinner Party by Judy Chicago

quotes: In a Franz Grillparzer play, this person orders the trusty servant Rhamnes to send away the slave Melitta
This figure believes that the slave Melitta has seduced the youth this figure loves, leading this figure to commit suicide

quotes: 'The Last Song of Sappho' is a poem from this author's Canti

clues.clue: Strabo credits invention of the seven-string lyre to a poet from Lesbos
quotes: Strabo credits the invention of the seven-string lyre to a poet from this island

cards.front: Poem: opens 'Deathless [goddess] of the spangled mind'; speaker asks the goddess to 'be my ally'
Poem: the goddess's chariot is drawn by sparrows (or doves); she is described as 'iridescent-throned'
Poem: the only complete surviving poem by an ancient Greek lyric poet from Lesbos
Poem: opens 'He seems to me equal to the gods'; the speaker becomes 'greener than grass' and feels 'thin fire racing under skin'
Poem: quoted by Longinus in On the Sublime as an example of literary intensity; adapted by Catullus as Poem 51
Poem: the speaker feels 'tongue breaks,' 'in eyes no sight,' 'drumming fills ears,' and 'cold sweat' while watching a man sit opposite a woman
Work: Anne Carson's translation uses square brackets as placeholders for lost or undecipherable words in ancient fragments; title comes from Fragment 22
Poem: rediscovered in 2014 by Dirk Obbink's team; centers on the poet's siblings Charaxos and Larichos
Poem: recalls the story of Tithonus and aging; states 'a human being without old age is not a possibility'; Carson translated it as 'The Beat Goes On'

cards.back: To an army wife in Sardis (Sappho)

cards.front: Poem: the speaker 'sleeps alone' when 'the moon and the Pleiades have set'; A.E. Housman adapted it
Poem: repeats 'O Hymenaeus!' and proclaims 'Lift high the roofbeam, carpenters'
Author: names a stanza form consisting of three hendecasyllable lines capped by a five-syllable Adonic line (dactyl + spondee)
Author: legend holds she threw herself from the Leucadian cliffs for the ferryman Phaon; story first appeared in a play by Menander
Author: Wilamowitz-Moellendorff theorized she was head of a girls' boarding school; exiled in Sicily according to the Marmor Parium
Author: Franz Grillparzer wrote a tragedy in which she orders servant Rhamnes to send away slave Melitta, then commits suicide
Author: Swinburne wrote the dramatic monologue 'Anactoria' from her perspective; speaker says 'My life is bitter with thy love'
Author: contemporary of Alcaeus of Mytilene, who praised her 'honey-sweet smile'; sole female member of the nine canonical Greek lyric poets

summary: Sappho is an ancient Greek lyric poet from the island of Lesbos, widely known as the 'Tenth Muse.' She is one of the nine canonical Greek lyric poets and its sole female member.
